candidate_law: When local competence reaches saturation without global robustness, successful rescue requires mechanisms aligned to the heterogeneous structure of family-level failure

rows.result_title: Strong local performance still fails compositionally
rows.comments: Direct support for the first half of the higher-order candidate

rows.result_title: Carry direction is causally important locally
rows.comments: Local mechanistic strength alone does not guarantee family-level explanation

rows.result_title: One family is trigger-correctable, another is not
rows.comments: Strong support for heterogeneous family-level failure

rows.result_title: Trajectory dynamics differ sharply across families
rows.comments: Strong support that family-level internal dynamics are not uniform

rows.result_title: Different family-level failures respond to different architectural components
Adaptive family-aware rescue outperforms naive rescue
Seen-family gain without broad held-out transfer
rows.comments: Supports narrow intervention gain pattern but not full architecture-level rescue

rows.result_title: Trajectory smoothing does not rescue failing family
rows.comments: Shows that not all rescue-like interventions are valid causal levers

notes: This is the first higher-order law test in Project 10.
It evaluates whether the full chain behind the candidate law is supported by the current program.
